lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: Nay, that’s right; but why wear you your leek today? Saint Davy’s day is past.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: There is occasions and causes why and wherefore in all things: I

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: will tell you, asse my friend, Captain Gower: the rascally, scauld, beggarly,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: lousy, pragging knave, Pistol, which you and yourself and all the world

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: know to be no petter than a fellow, look you now, of

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: no merits, he is come to me and prings me pread and

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: salt yesterday, look you, and bid me eat my leek: it was

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: in a place where I could not breed no contention with him;

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: but I will be so bold as to wear it in my

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: cap till I see him once again, and then I will tell

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: him a little piece of my desires.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: Why, here he comes, swelling like a turkey-cock.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: ’Tis no matter for his swellings nor his turkey-cocks. God pless you,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Aunchient Pistol! you scurvy, lousy knave, God pless you!

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Ha! art thou bedlam? dost thou thirst, base Trojan,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: To have me fold up Parca’s fatal web?

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Hence! I am qualmish at the smell of leek.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: I peseech you heartily, scurfy, lousy knave, at my desires, and my

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: requests, and my petitions, to eat, look you, this leek: because, look

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: you, you do not love it, nor your affections and your appetites

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: and your digestions doo’s not agree with it, I would desire you

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: to eat it.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Not for Cadwallader and all his goats.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: There is one goat for you. Will you be so good, scauld

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: knave, as eat it?

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Base Trojan, thou shalt die.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: You say very true, scauld knave, when God’s will is: I will

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: desire you to live in the meantime, and eat your victuals: come,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: there is sauce for it. You called me yesterday mountain-squire; but I

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: will make you today a squire of low degree. I pray you,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: fall to: if you can mock a leek, you can eat a

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: leek.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: Enough, captain: you have astonished him.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: I say, I will make him eat some part of my leek,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: or I will peat his pate four days. Bite, I pray you;

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: it is good for your green wound and your ploody coxcomb.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Must I bite?

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Yes, certainly, and out of doubt and out of question too, and ambiguities.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: By this leek, I will most horribly revenge: I eat and eat, I swear—

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Eat, I pray you: will you have some more sauce to your

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: leek? there is not enough leek to swear by.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Quiet thy cudgel; thou dost see I eat.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Much good do you, scauld knave, heartily. Nay, pray you, throw none

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: away; the skin is good for your broken coxcomb. When you take

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: occasions to see leeks hereafter, I pray you, mock at ’em; that

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: is all.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Good.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Ay, leeks is good: hold you, there is a groat to heal your pate.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Me a groat!

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: Yes, verily and in truth you, shall take it; or I have

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: another leek in my pocket, which you shall eat.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: I take thy groat in earnest of revenge.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: If I owe you anything I will pay you in cudgels: you

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: shall be a woodmonger, and buy nothing of me but cudgels. God

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: b’ wi’ you, and keep you, and heal your pate.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: All hell shall stir for this.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: Go, go; you are a counterfeit cowardly knave. Will you mock at

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: an ancient tradition, begun upon an honourable respect, and worn as a

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: memorable trophy of predeceased valour and dare not avouch in your deeds

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: any of your words? I have seen you gleeking and galling at

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: this gentleman twice or thrice. You thought, because he could not speak

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: English in the native garb, he could not therefore handle an English

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: cudgel: you find it otherwise; and henceforth let a Welsh correction teach

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: you a good English condition. Fare ye well.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Doth Fortune play the huswife with me now?

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: News have I, that my Doll is dead i’ the spital

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Of malady of France;

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: And there my rendezvous is quite cut off.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Old I do wax; and from my weary limbs

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Honour is cudgelled. Well, bawd I’ll turn,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: And something lean to cutpurse of quick hand.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: To England will I steal, and there I’ll steal:

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: And patches will I get unto these cudgell’d scars,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: And swear I got them in the Gallia wars.

glosses.definition: March 1 (the Welsh national day) has already passed — why still wearing the leek? (see 4.1 and 4.7)

glosses.phrase: occasions and causes why and wherefore
glosses.definition: Fluellen's pedantic triple-phrase for 'reasons'
scabby; scurvy (dialect)
bragging, in Fluellen's Welsh accent — dialect marker

glosses.phrase: Pistol, which you and yourself and all the world
glosses.definition: Fluellen's over-emphatic rhetoric
brings me bread (Welsh 'b' → 'p'); dialect preserved
Pistol earlier mocked the leek in front of Fluellen; now Fluellen will make him eat it, literally
puffed up like a turkey — Gower's classic English image of pomposity
'God bless you' in Welsh accent (sarcastic)
from 'Bethlem Hospital' — madhouse; Pistol calls Fluellen mad
vile scoundrel — Pistolese inflation
close up Fate's doomed tapestry — 'Parcae' = the three Roman Fates
nauseated
beseech you (Welsh 'b' → 'p')
Fluellen's catalogue style — listing inclinations exhaustively
the last British-Welsh king before the Saxons (7th century) — Pistol swears by a Welsh legend
Fluellen strikes Pistol with a cudgel; 'goat' plays on Pistol's 'goats'
mountain gentleman — a contemptuous term for a Welshman; Pistol had used it
lowly gentleman — Fluellen's deflating retort; a 15th-century romance title
beat your head for four days (Welsh 'b' → 'p')
bloody head (Welsh dialect); 'coxcomb' = head

glosses.phrase: not enough leek to swear by
glosses.definition: there's not enough leek left to use as an oath — Fluellen will keep feeding him
stout stick
a fourpenny coin — small money
a seller of firewood — Fluellen will only pay him in cudgels
fake, cowardly scoundrel — Gower's dressing-down of Pistol
the valour of earlier generations
mocking and jeering
English in the native manner — the 'standard' accent

glosses.phrase: Welsh correction teach you a good English condition
glosses.definition: Welsh discipline will teach you English manners — Gower's closing hit
harlot; strumpet — Pistol pictures Fortune as a whore who betrayed him

glosses.phrase: my Doll is dead i’ the spital
glosses.definition: Doll Tearsheet has died in the hospital — 'my Doll'; the Hostess died of the same disease as was rumored
syphilis — euphemistically called 'the French disease'
refuge; retreat (French term Pistol re-uses from 2.1)
I'll become a pimp — Pistol's decline to outright criminality
incline toward pickpocketing

glosses.phrase: patches will I get unto these cudgell’d scars
glosses.definition: I'll disguise my beating-bruises as war-wounds
the French wars — 'Gallia' = ancient Gaul

annotations.title: Fluellen forces Pistol to eat the leek — the cycle closes
annotations.body: The leek scene is long-setup comedy. At 4.1 Pistol mocked Fluellen's leek (not knowing he was speaking to the king's kinsman); at 5.1 Fluellen, now a veteran with Henry's acknowledgement, beats Pistol with a cudgel and forces him to eat a raw leek bite by bite. The comedy is brutal: Pistol is a known coward and a known petty criminal, and the scene gives us the satisfaction of seeing his bombast physically collapsed. But there is also a social point. Fluellen, the Welsh captain whose dialect we have been permitted to laugh at for four acts, now enforces discipline on the English braggart, and Gower (the English captain) endorses the act at length: 'henceforth let a Welsh correction teach you a good English condition.' The ethnic hierarchy the play has played with is, in this scene, upended: the Welshman teaches the Englishman manners. The scene also works as a final, unsentimental reckoning with the Eastcheap world. Pistol's closing soliloquy — Doll is dead, the rendezvous is cut off, 'bawd I'll turn, / And something lean to cutpurse of quick hand' — announces that he will go home to England and live by crime, with his 'cudgell'd scars' passed off as war-wounds. This is what the campaign has produced in the low-comic line: a criminal with a false story. The play does not soften it.
sources: Robert Weimann, Shakespeare and the Popular Tradition in the Theater (1978)
Ann Rosalind Jones, 'The Ethnographic Present,' in Shakespeare and the Early Modern (2002)

annotations.title: Pistol's homecoming as the play's underfoot pay-off
annotations.body: Pistol's short closing soliloquy is the play's only honest post-war economic statement. He reports two facts: his wife Doll/Nell has died of the 'malady of France' (syphilis), and his tavern-rendezvous is therefore cut off. His plan: steal his way home to England, become a pimp ('bawd') and cut-purse, pass off his leek-beating bruises as war-wounds ('the Gallia wars'). The grim comedy is that Pistol will be one of the veterans of Agincourt the English streets soon see. The King's 'band of brothers' promise — that shared combat would 'gentle' a low soldier's condition — is falsified here by a low soldier making his own account. The speech is less than a dozen lines; it is what the victory looks like from the side of the army that had nothing to begin with. Readers should notice that Shakespeare ends his low-plot with this speech and not with the leek's beating. The beating is the comic catharsis; the soliloquy is the audit.
sources: C. L. Barber, Shakespeare's Festive Comedy (1959)
Thomas Healy, Shakespeare, Alienation and the Early Modern Stage (1994)

trials.prompt: What does the leek scene accomplish?
options: A long-setup comic pay-off: Fluellen beats Pistol with a cudgel and forces him to eat a raw leek, inverting the play's ethnic hierarchy — the Welshman corrects the English bombast, with Gower's explicit endorsement

trials.prompt: What does Pistol's closing soliloquy reveal?
options: That he will return to England as a pimp and cut-purse, passing off his leek-bruises as war-wounds. His honest post-war account falsifies Henry's 'band of brothers' promise of social elevation for low-born soldiers
That he plans to marry Alice
That he has enlisted again for France

trials.prompt: What has happened to Pistol's wife?
options: She has died of the 'malady of France' — syphilis — in the hospital
